caveats: a: The occupation evidence covers a broader chemical-manufacturing group rather than NAICS 325220 alone.
a: Installed automation and task allocation vary strongly between melt spinning, solvent spinning, and texturizing operations.
rho: This is bounded judgment, not an observed fiber-industry adoption rate.
rho: EPA monitoring, testing, and recordkeeping obligations limit unattended automation in regulated acrylic and modacrylic processes.
e: No public dataset reports toll or contract share at six digits.
e: Dedicated customer specifications alone do not necessarily make a product sale an outsourced service.
s5: The age statistic is not industry- or size-specific and measures owners, not firms or exits.
s5: Plants owned by diversified groups have no individual-owner succession trigger.
q: No public contract set separates specialty from commodity programs.
q: Retention can differ sharply between high-specification technical fiber and standard polyester or nylon output.
d5: The production index is supply-side and not specific to contract producers.
d5: The 2017 comparison does not by itself determine the next five years and mixes fast-growing technical fibers with pressured commodity fibers.
o: Required operator quantity can migrate to larger or offshore plants even if the physical operator role persists.
o: This primitive does not duplicate labor automation, which is reflected in a and rho.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring industrial programs priced per pound, lot, or contracted capacity, with customer specifications for polymer, denier, color, finish, tow, staple, yarn, or filament performance and periodic raw-material or energy repricing.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS definition mixes broad product manufacturing with customer-specific production. The lens is narrowed to repeat contract, toll, and dedicated-program manufacturing so the screened firms share a coherent outsourced-service revenue model.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive integrated fiber plants, standard-product commodity sellers without an outsourced production relationship, texturizers of purchased fiber classified in NAICS 313110, glass-fiber manufacturers, distributors, shells, and non-control vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly producing or texturizing artificial and synthetic fibers or filaments to external-customer specifications under contract, tolling, or dedicated-program arrangements.

a.bridge: BLS identifies chemical equipment operators, production supervisors, chemical plant operators, industrial machinery mechanics, and other plant roles as the largest occupations across chemical manufacturing groups 3251, 3252, 3253, and 3259. I map record review, quality documentation, scheduling, production analysis, maintenance knowledge retrieval, procurement, and customer specification handling to AI exposure while treating line tending, spinning, material movement, repairs, and safety-critical control as predominantly physical. The broad chemical-manufacturing mix may overstate office-like work relative to fiber plants, biasing the estimate upward.
caveats: The occupation evidence covers a broader chemical-manufacturing group rather than NAICS 325220 alone.
Installed automation and task allocation vary strongly between melt spinning, solvent spinning, and texturizing operations.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task census from contract fiber plants, with time studies for laboratory release, line scheduling, specification management, maintenance planning, and environmental reporting, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: Continuous spinning and texturizing remain equipment-intensive, but a material wage-weighted layer of quality, planning, laboratory interpretation, maintenance coordination, compliance, and commercial administration is exposed to AI assistance or avoided hiring.

d5.bridge: The Federal Reserve industrial-production index measures real US output for NAICS 32522, not constant-quality demand for the outsourced subset. The index stood at 71.8055 in June 2026 versus a 2017 base of 100, indicating a materially smaller domestic production level. I use that structural weakness as a downward demand anchor while allowing stabilization or recovery. Import substitution can make domestic production fall faster than underlying customer consumption, so this proxy can understate demand.
caveats: The production index is supply-side and not specific to contract producers.
The 2017 comparison does not by itself determine the next five years and mixes fast-growing technical fibers with pressured commodity fibers.
d5.change_evidence: US apparent consumption by fiber type, customer production forecasts, import penetration, plant closure announcements, and contracted-volume backlogs for qualifying producers would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Artificial and synthetic fibers have diverse apparel, furnishing, carpet, carbon-fiber, and industrial uses, but the domestic output index indicates sustained competitive pressure. The base assumes further moderate contraction rather than disappearance.

caveats: No public dataset reports toll or contract share at six digits.
Dedicated customer specifications alone do not necessarily make a product sale an outsourced service.
e.change_evidence: A census of the injected LMM firms coded from plant capabilities, customer-program language, tolling contracts, and management interviews would directly resolve eligibility.
e.rationale: The industry explicitly includes both manufacturing and manufacturing-plus-texturizing, but public classification does not distinguish standard proprietary products from recurring customer-specific contract programs. A minority, rather than most firms, is likely to meet the strict outsourced-service lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325220 ($2,490,188,630) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325220 ($7,367,673,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $505,848 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325220); class <5: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,882,875 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $339,106 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,201,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $396,520 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 7 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,242,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $764,061 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,897,517 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,223,343 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $61,772,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,125,257 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $237,903,522 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $42,846,424 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 29

caveats: Required operator quantity can migrate to larger or offshore plants even if the physical operator role persists.
This primitive does not duplicate labor automation, which is reflected in a and rho.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers broadly insourcing spinning, material substitution eliminating fiber programs, or certified autonomous plants operating without an accountable third-party manufacturer would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Any remaining physical fiber quantity must be polymerized or dissolved, spun, finished, tested, and released by an accountable manufacturer. Software may automate decisions and records but cannot supply the filament or assume environmental and product-quality accountability.

caveats: No public contract set separates specialty from commodity programs.
Retention can differ sharply between high-specification technical fiber and standard polyester or nylon output.
q.change_evidence: Representative customer contracts showing open-book pricing, resin and energy escalators, renewal frequency, cost-down clauses, minimum volumes, and rebid outcomes would materially change this range.
q.rationale: Customer qualification, specialty formulations, color and finish consistency, and switching disruption support retention, while per-pound contracts, commodity polymer benchmarks, annual cost-down requests, overcapacity, and import competition pass savings to buyers.

caveats: This is bounded judgment, not an observed fiber-industry adoption rate.
EPA monitoring, testing, and recordkeeping obligations limit unattended automation in regulated acrylic and modacrylic processes.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at fiber plants showing accepted AI recommendations, labor hours removed, defect outcomes, override rates, and deployment time would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Five years is sufficient for copilots in quality records, specification search, scheduling, maintenance, and compliance, but integration with plant historians and laboratory systems, cyber controls, process validation, and workforce adoption limit realized implementation.

s5.bridge: Census found 51% of responding US employer-business owners were age 55 or older in 2018. I convert broad aging pressure to a five-year probability of a qualifying control transfer and haircut for corporate ownership, family succession, strategic consolidation, closures, and the specialized capital and environmental diligence of fiber plants. The broad all-industry statistic probably overstates retirement-driven transfers in capital-intensive chemical manufacturing.
caveats: The age statistic is not industry- or size-specific and measures owners, not firms or exits.
Plants owned by diversified groups have no individual-owner succession trigger.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, or GF Data transactions for US specialty fiber and filament producers, reconciled to current beneficial ownership and owner age, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Succession pressure is real, but only a subset produces an arm's-length control sale of an eligible operating company within five years.

principal_driver: Repeat customer specifications create valuable AI-assisted quality, planning, maintenance, and documentation workflows around unavoidable physical production.
principal_weakness: Domestic fiber output is structurally weak and the true contract-manufacturing subset is not publicly measured.

run_meta.title: Artificial and Synthetic Fibers and Filaments Manufacturing

sources.supports: Defines the industry as cellulosic and noncellulosic fiber and filament manufacturing, including manufacturing and texturizing, and distinguishes texturizing fibers made elsewhere.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 325220 Artificial and Synthetic Fibers and Filaments Manufacturing

sources.supports: Lists chemical equipment operators, production supervisors, chemical plant operators, industrial machinery mechanics, and related plant roles among the largest occupations in chemical manufacturing groups including 3252.
sources.title: Data tables for OEWS industry charts, May 2024

sources.supports: Reports that production occupations were about half of manufacturing jobs in 2024 and that continued automated-machinery adoption increases need for mechanics.
sources.title: Producing the goods of the future: Job opportunities in manufacturing

sources.supports: Describes finished fiber forms and melt, dry-solvent, wet-solvent, and reaction spinning methods across polyester, nylon, polyolefin, acrylic, modacrylic, spandex, and rayon.
sources.title: AP-42 Section 6.9 Synthetic Fibers

sources.supports: States fiber composition thresholds, end uses, and standards for process vents, spinning lines, storage, wastewater, and equipment leaks, including testing, monitoring, and recordkeeping.
sources.title: Acrylic and Modacrylic Fibers Production NESHAP for Area Sources

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 ABS, data year 2018.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages: Over Half of U.S. Business Owners Were Age 55 and Over

sources.supports: Reports the Federal Reserve real-output index at 71.8055 in June 2026, with 2017 equal to 100, and identifies the series as monthly US industrial production.
sources.title: Industrial Production: Artificial and Synthetic Fibers and Filaments (NAICS 32522)

title: Artificial and Synthetic Fibers and Filaments Manufacturing
